days.headline: First stage back after the rest day, Aurillac to Le Lioran — steep, irregular Cantal climbs that suit an ambush more than a set-piece mountain train.

picks.why: The dominant climber of the race so far and the rider most likely to seize on any hesitation from rivals fresh off the rest day.
Has stayed glued to the front of the GC group in the mountains and is a threat to counter-attack on steep, irregular gradients.
Climbed strongly through the Pyrenean queen stage and is built for exactly this kind of unpredictable medium-mountain terrain.

days.headline: A gentle, short run north along the Allier valley to Nevers — sprinters' day, with a fast, controlled race expected.

picks.why: Won the Bordeaux sprint and is the man to beat whenever the race comes back together in a straight fight.
Podiumed behind Merlier in Bordeaux and has the top-end speed to go one better.
Runner-up in Bordeaux and in career-best sprint form.

days.headline: A novelty start at Magny-Cours then east across open Burgundy farmland to Chalon-sur-Saône — another sprint day, but crosswinds could split the bunch.

picks.why: The in-form sprinter of the race; only a split bunch would seriously trouble him.
Consistently in the mix in the bunch sprints and dangerous if the wind picks up and echelons form.
Well-drilled leadout that holds up in a straight fight to the line.

days.headline: The longest stage of the race skirts the edge of the Jura to Belfort — distance plus constant rolling terrain makes this a strongman's breakaway day.

picks.why: A durable puncheur who can survive a long day out front in the escape.
Loves a long, rolling breakaway stage exactly like this one.
A classics powerhouse capable of forcing the winning move on a long, punchy stage.

days.headline: A dense Vosges mountain stage into Le Markstein Fellering — the gradients aren't Alpine but the climbs come relentlessly, punishing any off day.

picks.why: The strongest climber in the race and the rider best placed to exploit a repeatedly punchy finale like this.
The clearest GC threat to Pogačar so far and a rider who thrives when climbs come back-to-back rather than in one long haul.
The form of the Tour's breakout young climber suits a day where repeated steep pitches matter more than raw altitude.

fantasy.strategy: With the stage 8-14 transfer window still open, prioritise a climber like Pogačar, Vingegaard, or del Toro for the ambush-prone Le Lioran stage and the punchy Le Markstein finale, since climbing days outscore sprint days in this format. Rotate in a pure sprinter (Merlier is the clear pick) for the flatter, shorter days into Nevers and Chalon-sur-Saône, and hold a breakaway-capable puncheur (Turgis or Godon) in reserve for the long, rolling Belfort stage, which offers no clean sprint finish. Save the double-points bonus stage for a summit finish later in the race rather than spending it in this mixed block.
